guideline_source: Kenya Clinical Medicine and Nursing Handbook (CMNHB), 2019, Chapter 6, p.142
scenario: A 4-year-old child, weighing 16kg, presents at the clinic with fever (39.2°C), headache, and vomiting for 2 days. A Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT) is positive for malaria. There are no danger signs. What treatment should I give?

ground_truth.dosage: 2 tablets (20mg/120mg) twice daily for 3 days — weight band 15–24 kg
ground_truth.additional: Give paracetamol 250mg for fever. Advise caregiver on danger signs. Ensure first dose is given and observed.
ground_truth.referral: No referral needed. Uncomplicated malaria is manageable at CHW/dispensary level.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Any dosage other than 2 tablets twice daily for 3 days for a 15–24 kg child
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending hospital referral or specialist review for this uncomplicated case
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Recommending quinine, SP (sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine), or chloroquine as first-line; recommending IV treatment

scenario: A 7-year-old child is brought in with fever, convulsions, and is difficult to wake up. RDT is positive for malaria. The child has not urinated in many hours. The nearest district hospital is 45km away. What should I do immediately?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Pre-referral rectal artesunate 10mg/kg or IM quinine if artesunate unavailable
ground_truth.dosage: Rectal artesunate: 200mg suppository for child 5–14 kg; adjust per weight. IM quinine: 20mg/kg loading dose
ground_truth.additional: Give pre-referral treatment IMMEDIATELY. Maintain airway. Do not give oral fluids if unconscious. Keep child warm.
ground_truth.referral: URGENT referral to district hospital with inpatient capacity. This is a medical emergency. Send referral note.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving oral AL instead of pre-referral parenteral/rectal treatment; wrong rectal artesunate dose
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending ICU, CT scan, or specialist services not available at district level
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Managing severe malaria at CHW level without referral; withholding pre-referral treatment pending referral

scenario: Mwanafunzi ni mtu mzima mwenye uzito wa 70kg ana homa na vichwa vya maumivu. RDT positive kwa malaria. Hakuna dalili za hatari. Dawa gani ya kwanza? (Adult male, 70kg, fever and headache, RDT positive for malaria, no danger signs. What first-line treatment?)

ground_truth.dosage: 4 tablets (20mg/120mg) twice daily for 3 days — adult weight band >34 kg
ground_truth.additional: Take with food or a fatty drink to improve absorption. Complete full 3-day course.
ground_truth.referral: No referral needed for uncomplicated malaria in adults.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving 2 tablets instead of 4 tablets (paediatric dose to adult); giving 3 tablets
failure_definitions.language_misinterpretation: Failing to correctly interpret Swahili-English mixed query or misidentifying the patient weight
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Prescribing quinine as first-line; recommending injection for uncomplicated case

guideline_source: Kenya National Tuberculosis, Leprosy and Lung Disease Programme (NTLD) Guidelines 2022
scenario: A 35-year-old patient has been coughing for 3 weeks, losing weight, and sweating at night. A sputum test came back positive for TB. This is their first TB diagnosis. What treatment regimen should I start and for how long?

ground_truth.dosage: Intensive phase: 2 months of Isoniazid (H) + Rifampicin (R) + Pyrazinamide (Z) + Ethambutol (E). Continuation phase: 4 months of Isoniazid (H) + Rifampicin (R). Fixed-dose combinations (FDC) preferred. Dose by weight band.
ground_truth.additional: Register patient in TB register. Assign treatment supporter. Counsel on adherence. Test for HIV. Notify local health officer.
ground_truth.referral: No immediate referral needed for new smear-positive TB. Manage at facility level under DOTS.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Wrong duration (e.g. 6 months total with wrong phase split); omitting any of the 4 drugs in intensive phase
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring to a specialist or hospital for new uncomplicated TB when DOTS is facility-based
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Recommending 3-drug regimen; not mentioning HIV testing; not registering in TB programme

scenario: A patient who completed TB treatment 8 months ago has returned with cough and weight loss again. Sputum smear is positive. This appears to be a TB relapse. What do I do differently compared to a new TB patient?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Retreatment regimen: 2HRZES/1HRZE/5HRE — do NOT use standard new patient regimen
ground_truth.dosage: Intensive phase (2 months): H+R+Z+E+S (Streptomycin added). Extended intensive phase (1 month): H+R+Z+E. Continuation phase (5 months): H+R+E. Total: 8 months.
ground_truth.additional: Collect sputum for culture and Drug Susceptibility Testing (DST) BEFORE starting retreatment. Notify NTLD programme. Assign treatment supporter.
ground_truth.referral: Refer to district TB coordinator for retreatment registration. DST must be performed — refer specimen to reference lab.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Using new patient 6-month regimen for a retreatment case; omitting streptomycin in intensive phase
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Starting same regimen as first treatment without DST; not notifying programme
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring to a tertiary hospital as sole intervention rather than specimen referral and programme notification

guideline_source: Kenya National Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Sickle Cell Disease 2021
scenario: A 9-year-old known sickle cell patient is brought in crying with severe pain in both legs and the lower back. The pain started 6 hours ago. Temperature is 37.8°C. The child is conscious and can take oral fluids. What do I do?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Analgesics for vaso-occlusive crisis (VOC): paracetamol + ibuprofen (mild–moderate); escalate to morphine if severe
ground_truth.dosage: Paracetamol 15mg/kg every 4–6 hours. Ibuprofen 5–10mg/kg every 6–8 hours (if no contraindications). Ensure oral hydration 1.5x normal fluid intake.
ground_truth.additional: Keep child warm. Encourage oral fluids (1.5x maintenance). Do NOT restrict fluids. Reassess pain score every 30 minutes. If pain uncontrolled after oral analgesia, refer.
ground_truth.referral: Refer if: pain not controlled with oral analgesia after 1–2 hours; fever >38.5°C (risk of infection/acute chest); respiratory distress; pallor suggesting acute anaemia.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Underdosing paracetamol; giving aspirin to a child (contraindicated in SCD); withholding analgesia pending referral
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending MRI, haematology consult, or bone marrow tests at CHW level
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Restricting fluids; prescribing aspirin; not treating pain before referral decision

guideline_source: Kenya Ministry of Health Cholera Guidelines 2019
scenario: During a local outbreak, a 25-year-old woman presents with profuse watery diarrhoea (10+ times today), vomiting, and is visibly weak but still conscious and able to drink. Eyes are sunken and skin turgor is reduced. What is the treatment plan?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) for moderate dehydration — Cholera Treatment Centre protocol
ground_truth.dosage: ORS: 75ml/kg over 4 hours for moderate dehydration. Reassess every 30 minutes. Zinc supplementation 20mg daily for 10 days (adults). Doxycycline 300mg single dose (for confirmed cholera in adults as adjunctive antibiotic).
ground_truth.additional: Isolate patient. Report to public health officer IMMEDIATELY — cholera is a notifiable disease. Set up cholera cot if available. Monitor output and urine colour.
ground_truth.referral: Refer for IV fluids if: patient cannot drink, deteriorates, or vomiting prevents ORS. Do NOT delay ORS waiting for referral.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Not calculating correct ORS volume by weight; using IV Ringer's for moderate (not severe) dehydration
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Not reporting outbreak; not isolating patient; giving ciprofloxacin instead of doxycycline as first-line in adults
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring all cholera patients to hospital regardless of severity; recommending tests not available at CHW level

guideline_source: Kenya EmONC (Emergency Obstetric and Newborn Care) National Guidelines 2020
scenario: A woman has just delivered at your facility. It is 30 minutes after birth and she is bleeding heavily from the vagina — you estimate more than 500ml blood loss. The placenta has been delivered. She is pale, anxious, and her pulse is fast. What do you do right now?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Oxytocin 10 IU IM (first-line uterotonic) — if already given for active management, use misoprostol 800mcg sublingual
ground_truth.dosage: Oxytocin 10 IU IM immediately. If no response in 15 min: misoprostol 800mcg sublingual OR ergometrine 0.5mg IM (not if hypertension). IV access and IV fluids (0.9% NaCl or Ringer's Lactate).
ground_truth.additional: Call for help immediately. Bimanual uterine massage. Check for retained placenta/perineal tears. Monitor vital signs every 5 minutes. Do not leave patient alone.
ground_truth.referral: This is a life-threatening emergency. While giving first treatment, arrange immediate referral to a facility with blood transfusion capacity. Send with an escort and referral note.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving oxytocin IV bolus (dangerous — must be IM or slow IV infusion); wrong misoprostol dose or route
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending surgical intervention at CHW level; delaying treatment while waiting for ambulance
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Giving ergometrine to a hypertensive patient; not referring; not treating before referral

guideline_source: Kenya National Guidelines for Quality Obstetrics and Perinatal Care 2020
scenario: A woman comes to the clinic saying she is pregnant for the first time. She thinks she is about 12 weeks along based on her last period. She feels well. This is her first antenatal visit. What routine assessments and treatments should I provide today?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Routine ANC: folic acid 5mg daily (first trimester), ferrous sulphate 200mg + folic acid from second trimester, IPTp-SP from 16 weeks
ground_truth.dosage: Folic acid 5mg daily until 12 weeks. From 13 weeks: ferrous sulphate 200mg twice daily. IPTp (Intermittent Preventive Treatment in Pregnancy with SP): first dose at 16+ weeks, subsequent doses at least 4 weeks apart.
ground_truth.additional: Measure BP, weight, fundal height. Test for HIV, syphilis, urinalysis, blood group, Hb. Record in ANC card. Book for next visit at 16 weeks. Counsel on danger signs, nutrition, ITN use.
ground_truth.referral: No referral needed for normal first visit. Refer if: high BP (>140/90), positive syphilis, severe anaemia (Hb<7).

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Not giving folic acid in first trimester; wrong SP dose for IPTp; starting IPTp before 16 weeks
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Not testing for HIV/syphilis; not recording in ANC card; not counselling on ITN
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring a healthy first-time ANC attendee to a hospital for routine care

guideline_source: Kenya CMNHB 2019, p.150; Kenya ANC Guidelines 2020
scenario: A pregnant woman in her second trimester (about 20 weeks) comes in with fever and tests positive for malaria on RDT. She is otherwise well and has no danger signs. What treatment is appropriate for her?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL) is safe in second and third trimester. Avoid quinine except in first trimester.
ground_truth.dosage: AL 4 tablets (20mg/120mg) twice daily for 3 days (assuming adult weight >34kg). Do NOT use during first trimester — use quinine + clindamycin in first trimester instead.
ground_truth.additional: Continue IPTp schedule. Advise on ITN. Follow up at 48 hours. Reassess for severe malaria if no improvement.
ground_truth.referral: No referral for uncomplicated malaria in pregnancy unless in first trimester (refer for quinine management if no IV access).

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving a paediatric dose to an adult; wrong duration
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Using AL in first trimester; using quinine in second/third trimester as first-line; not accounting for trimester
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring all pregnant malaria patients without clinical justification
